description.en: Complete guide to bolt tightening and torque specifications. Learn proper torque values, tightening methods, and preload calculation for reliable bolted joints.
description.zh: 螺栓紧固和扭矩规格完整指南。了解正确的扭矩值、紧固方法和预紧力计算，以获得可靠的螺栓连接。
description.es: Guía completa de especificaciones de par de apriete de pernos. Aprenda valores de torque, métodos de apriete y cálculo de precarga.

description.hi: बोल्ट टाइटनिंग और टॉर्क स्पेसिफिकेशन की पूर्ण गाइड। उचित टॉर्क वैल्यू, टाइटनिंग विधियां, और विश्वसनीय बोल्टेड जॉइंट के लिए प्रीलोड कैलकुलेशन सीखें।

keywords: bolt torque, tightening methods, preload, torque specification, bolted joint, torque wrench

body.en:
Proper bolt tightening creates clamp force (preload) that holds the joint together:

Torque Formula:
T = K × F × d

Where:
- T = Torque (Nm or ft-lb)
- K = Nut factor (typically 0.2)
- F = Desired preload (N or lb)
- d = Nominal bolt diameter (m or ft)

Preload Requirements:
- Joint design determines required preload
- Too little: Joint may separate
- Too much: Bolt may yield or fail

Typical preload: 60-75% of bolt proof load

Typical torque values for metric hex bolts: Grade 8.8 Bolts: - M8: 25-30 Nm - M10: 50-60 Nm - M12: 85-95 Nm - M16: 210-240 Nm - M20: 410-460 Nm - M24: 710-800 Nm Grade 10.9 Bolts: - M8: 35-40 Nm - M10: 70-80 Nm - M12: 120-135 Nm - M16: 295-330 Nm - M20: 580-650 Nm - M24: 1000-1100 Nm Note: Values are approximate and depend on lubrication condition.

Different methods for achieving proper preload: 1. Torque Wrench Method: - Most common method - Direct torque measurement - Accuracy ±10-20% - Requires calibrated wrench 2. Angle Turn Method: - Turn bolt specific angle after snug - More accurate than torque - Requires experienced operator - Common: 60-90° after snug 3. Hydraulic Tensioner: - Uses hydraulic pressure - Very accurate preload - Used for large bolts (M36+) - Expensive equipment 4. Combined Method: - Torque to 50-60% of target - Angle turn remaining amount - Best accuracy and repeatability

q.en: What torque should I use for M10 bolt Grade 8.8?

a.en: For M10 Grade 8.8 hex bolts, the recommended torque is 50-60 Nm (37-44 ft-lb). For Grade 10.9, use 70-80 Nm (52-59 ft-lb). Always refer to your specific application requirements.

q.en: What is the difference between preload and torque?

a.en: Torque is the rotational force applied to tighten a bolt. Preload (clamp force) is the tension created in the bolt when tightened. The goal is to achieve correct preload; torque is just the method to reach it.

q.en: Can torque specification bolts be reused after loosening?

a.en: Generally no. Once a bolt has been torqued beyond its yield point and then loosened, it may have been permanently stretched. Replace with new bolts for critical structural applications.

q.en: What is the nut factor (K) in torque calculation?

a.en: The nut factor K accounts for friction in the torque-tension relationship. K = 0.2 is commonly used for plain (unlubricated) bolts. For lubricated bolts, K may be 0.15-0.18. Always check with your specific coating/lubrication condition.

q.en: How do I know if a bolt is properly tightened?

a.en: Use a calibrated torque wrench set to the correct specification. For critical joints, use direct tension indicators (washers with raised bumps) or ultrasonic bolt stretch measurement.

metaDescription: Proper bolt tightening methods, torque specifications, preload control, and installation best practices. Includes torque charts for Grade 8.8 and 10.9 bolts.

faqItems.question: What is the correct torque specification for Grade 8.8 bolts?
faqItems.answer: Grade 8.8 torque values (Nm) for lubricated bolts: M6=10Nm, M8=25Nm, M10=49Nm, M12=85Nm, M16=210Nm, M20=410Nm, M24=710Nm. For plain (unlubricated) bolts, increase by 25%. These values are approximate - always refer to specific torque charts for your application and consider friction coefficients.

faqItems.question: How do I calculate torque for a specific bolt application?
faqItems.answer: Torque calculation formula: T = K × F × d where: T = torque (Nm); K = friction coefficient (typically 0.2 for lubricated, 0.3 for plain); F = desired preload (N) = σ × As (stress × stress area); d = nominal bolt diameter (m). For African mining equipment, always use manufacturer-specified torque values with certified torque charts.

faqItems.question: What happens if bolts are over-tightened or under-tightened?
faqItems.answer: Over-tightening risks: Bolt yield or fracture; Thread stripping; Gasket crushing; Distortion of joint components; Catastrophic failure in service. Under-tightening risks: Joint separation under load; Bolt fatigue from dynamic loads; Vibration-induced loosening; Leakage in flanged joints; Pre-mature failure in critical applications.

faqItems.question: What is the difference between torque tensioning and torque-to-yield?
faqItems.answer: Torque tensioning (standard method): Applies predetermined torque value regardless of actual preload - simple but affected by friction. Torque-to-yield (TTB): Tightens to specific bolt stretch (yield point), more accurate preload control - used for critical structural connections like桥梁 and mining equipment. TTB requires careful control and skilled technicians.

faqItems.question: How do I use a torque wrench correctly?
faqItems.answer: Torque wrench usage: 1) Select correct torque value for bolt grade and size; 2) Set wrench to desired value; 3) Apply smooth, steady pull - avoid jerky movements; 4) Listen/feel for click or release; 5) Never use as a breaker bar; 6) Calibrate annually. For African field conditions, use dial-type or click-type wrenches - digital requires battery management.
